title: Norwegian Campaign (Operation Weserübung)
summary: Operation Weserübung was the German invasion of Denmark and Norway, launched simultaneously on 9 April 1940. Despite British and French intervention and fierce Norwegian resistance, Germany secured both countries within two months, gaining strategically vital North Sea bases and iron ore transit routes.
context: The German occupation of Norway was driven by two strategic imperatives: securing the iron ore route from the Swedish mines at Kiruna (shipped via the Norwegian port of Narvik, then through neutral Norwegian waters) and preventing Britain from using Norway as a base to threaten Germany's northern flank. Grand Admiral Raeder had been pressing for a Norwegian operation since October 1939. The British had been planning their own mining of Norwegian waters (Operation Wilfred) — both operations launched almost simultaneously on 9 April, with Germany moving hours before the British. The simultaneous assault on Denmark (completed in a single day) and on six Norwegian ports via naval and airborne forces was an unprecedented combined-arms operation at extreme range. The Kriegsmarine paid a heavy price — three cruisers and ten destroyers sunk — damage that crippled German surface fleet operations for years. British and French forces landed at Narvik and Namsos but were outfought by German mountain troops and forced to re-embark in June. The campaign contributed directly to Neville Chamberlain's resignation and Churchill's appointment as Prime Minister on 10 May 1940.
notable: The campaign saw the first major combat use of Fallschirmjäger, seizing Fornebu airport at Oslo. The Kriegsmarine suffered losses that permanently constrained future surface fleet operations. The British failure triggered Chamberlain's fall and Churchill's appointment as Prime Minister.
outcome: German victory. Norway and Denmark occupied; Norwegian forces continued resistance until formal German surrender in May 1945, the last German garrison in Europe to surrender.

casualties.germany: ~5,296 killed, wounded, missing at sea; 3 cruisers and 10 destroyers sunk
casualties.allied: ~5,500 killed; Norwegian merchant navy suffered continuous losses throughout occupation

sources.ref: National Archives (UK). ADM 199/474: Naval Operations — Norway Campaign, April–June 1940.
Derry, T.K. The Campaign in Norway. HMSO, 1952.
sources.note: The official British military history of the Norwegian campaign.
Draws on German, British, Norwegian, and French records.

sources.ref: Ziemke, Earl. The German Northern Theater of Operations, 1940–1945. Department of the Army, 1959.
